name: Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park Loop from Cabra
name_original: Parque Natural Sierras Subbéticas — circular desde Cabra
description: This difficult 48.6-mile road cycling route from Cabra in Parque Natural Sierras Subbeticas offers dramatic scenery and challenging climbs.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Cabra are set within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, a region characterized by its rugged limestone formations and varied terrain. The landscape features significant elevation changes, offering challenging climbs and descents. Cyclists can explore routes that traverse through natural parks, past historical landmarks, and along former railway lines. The area provides a diverse backdrop for road cycling, from demanding mountain roads to more accessible paths. Best road cycling routes around Cabra The most popular road cycling route is Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park Loop from Cabra, a 48.5 miles (78.1 km) difficult trail that takes 4 hours 10 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Fuente del Río – Venta Los Pelaos loop from Cabra, a moderate 22.6 miles (36.3 km) path. This loop explores the area around the Fuente del Río, offering a mix of terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Fuente del Río – Sima Viaduct loop from Cabra, a 31.7 miles (50.9 km) trail leading through the Fuente del Río area and past the Sima Viaduct, often completed in about 2 hours 45 minutes. Road cycling around Cabra is defined by the rugged terrain of the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, limestone formations, and the Via Verde del Aceite. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to difficult climbs. The routes in Cabra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cabra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How challenging is road cycling in Cabra?
A: Road cycling in Cabra offers a diverse range of challenges, primarily due to its location within the rugged Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park. The terrain is characterized by limestone formations and significant elevation changes, providing both demanding climbs and exhilarating descents. While there are no routes classified as 'easy', you'll find 14 moderate routes and 19 difficult routes, ensuring options for various fitness levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while road cycling around Cabra?
A: You can expect dramatic and varied landscapes while road cycling around Cabra. Routes often traverse the &lt;strong&gt;Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;, a UNESCO Geopark, featuring rugged limestone formations, dolines, poljes, and extensive cave systems. You'll also encounter picturesque countryside, especially along the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31889323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Río – Sima Viaduct loop from Cabra&lt;/a&gt;, and sections of the &lt;strong&gt;Via Verde del Aceite&lt;/strong&gt;, an old railway line converted into a cycling path.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the road cycling routes in Cabra?
A: Yes, several routes pass by significant landmarks. For instance, the &lt;strong&gt;Via Verde del Aceite&lt;/strong&gt; near Zuheros offers impressive views of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5361618" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zuheros Castle&lt;/a&gt;, dramatically integrated into the limestone landscape. You might also encounter the serene source of the Cabra River, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31867386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Río&lt;/a&gt;, or even catch glimpses of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4741710" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of Zuheros Castle from the viaduct&lt;/a&gt;. The region is rich in natural and historical attractions.

Q: Can I find family-friendly road cycling options in Cabra?
A: While the immediate area around Cabra within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park is known for its challenging terrain, the nearby &lt;strong&gt;Via Verde del Aceite&lt;/strong&gt; offers a much gentler, family-friendly cycling experience. This Greenway, built on an old railway line, provides easy cycling through scenic countryside, making it suitable for families looking for less strenuous rides.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Cabra?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Cabra are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1008803262/sierras-subbeticas-natural-park-loop-from-cabra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park Loop from Cabra&lt;/a&gt;, the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31867386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Río – Venta Los Pelaos loop from Cabra&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31889323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente del Río – Sima Viaduct loop from Cabra&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Cabra?
A: The best time for road cycling in Cabra is generally during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is typically milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of the Andalusian summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter. This allows for more comfortable exploration of the region's varied terrain.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Cabra?
A: Cabra, as a town, offers various public parking options. For specific trailheads, it's advisable to check the starting points of individual routes on komoot. Many routes, being loops, often start and end in or near the town center or accessible points within the natural park, where parking facilities are usually available.

Q: How can I reach the road cycling trails in Cabra using public transport?
A: Cabra is accessible by public transport, primarily bus services connecting it to larger cities in Andalusia. Once in Cabra, many road cycling routes start directly from the town, making them accessible on two wheels. For routes deeper within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, you might need to cycle a short distance from the town center to reach the specific trailheads.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Cabra?
A: The road cycling routes in Cabra are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Over 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the dramatic scenery, challenging climbs, and the diverse terrain offered by the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes in Cabra for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking longer distances and significant elevation, Cabra offers several challenging options. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1294296474/sierras-subbeticas-natural-park-loop-from-cabra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park Loop from Cabra&lt;/a&gt; covers over 100 km with more than 2,100 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38194776" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro del Río Castle – Cabra loop from Cabra&lt;/a&gt;, which is over 115 km long with substantial climbing.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Given Cabra's location within a natural park and its proximity to several villages, you will find opportunities for refreshments. Routes that pass through or near towns like Zuheros or other settlements within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park often have cafes, pubs, or small shops where cyclists can refuel. It's always a good idea to check the route details on komoot for specific amenities along your chosen path.

Q: Which routes offer the most panoramic views in Cabra?
A: For breathtaking panoramic views, consider routes that ascend to higher elevations within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park. The park's highest point, &lt;strong&gt;El Picacho&lt;/strong&gt;, often referred to as 'Andalucia's Balcony', offers views encompassing five Andalusian provinces. While not a direct cycling route, roads leading up into the higher parts of the Sierra de Cabra will provide similar expansive vistas. The &lt;strong&gt;Via Verde del Aceite&lt;/strong&gt; also offers impressive views, particularly from the viaduct near Zuheros overlooking the castle.
tips: This town is considered the gateway to the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park. Its economy is mainly based on olive cultivation. Among the places of interest are: the castle, the Municipal Archaeological Museum, the Church of the Assumption and Angels.
This hermitage is located at the top of the Picacho de la Sierra de Cabra. From the height of 1200 masl you have spectacular views of the mountains and the countryside.
Picturesque town on the edge of the Parque Natural de las Sierras Subbéticas. From the Plaza de Santa Maria you have a magnificent view of the surroundings.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This challenging racebike route, covering approximately 78 kilometers with nearly 1,500 meters of elevation gain, typically takes around 4 hours and 10 minutes to complete. It requires very good fitness due to its demanding climbs.

faqs.question: Is this racebike route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and is not suitable for beginners. It features significant elevation changes and demanding climbs characteristic of the Sierras Subbéticas, an area known for its challenging terrain, even featuring in Spain's 'La Vuelta' cycling tour.

faqs.question: Where does this racebike route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: This is a circular route starting and ending in Cabra. As a popular starting point for routes in the Parque Natural Sierras Subbéticas, Cabra offers various parking options within the town, making it convenient to access the trailhead.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is characterized by a dramatic limestone karst landscape with rugged features and rolling hills. While most roads are well-maintained, expect significant elevation changes and demanding climbs. The region is known for its challenging routes, requiring good fitness.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll be treated to spectacular mountain scenery, picturesque white villages like Zuheros, and extensive olive groves. The route offers breathtaking views of limestone formations, deep gorges, and river valleys, especially around notable peaks and the Hermitage of Our Lady of the Sierra.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The spring and autumn months are generally ideal for cycling in the Sierras Subbéticas, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant natural beauty, including an explosion of wildflowers in spring. Summers can be very hot, and winters may bring cooler, wetter conditions.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or villages will I pass on this route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Cabra and passes through the charming white village of Zuheros. You'll also encounter the significant Hermitage of Our Lady of the Sierra, offering panoramic views.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite and also passes through parts of [CIMA CO03] Virgen de la Sierra and Camino Mozárabe desde Málaga.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: While the Parque Natural Sierras Subbéticas generally allows dogs, this specific route is designed for racebikes on roads. It's best to keep dogs on a leash in natural areas and be mindful of traffic on shared roads. Consider if the distance and difficulty are appropriate for your dog if they are accompanying you.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for the Parque Natural Sierras Subbéticas?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route within the Parque Natural Sierras Subbéticas. The park is a UNESCO Geopark, emphasizing its geological richness and natural heritage, and is freely accessible for outdoor activities like cycling.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like cafes or accommodation near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the region is well-equipped for cycling tourism. Both Cabra and Zuheros offer cafes, restaurants, and bike-friendly accommodation options. You'll find places to refuel and rest before or after your ride.

faqs.question: What makes the Sierras Subbéticas an 'undiscovered gem' for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: The Sierras Subbéticas is considered an 'undiscovered gem' due to its combination of challenging terrain, spectacular scenery, and remarkably quiet roads with very little traffic. This allows cyclists to fully immerse themselves in the natural beauty and demanding climbs without constant interruption, making for a truly unique experience.

waypoints.name: Hermitage of Our Lady of the Sierra
